title: Lesson 433
transcript: We just talked about ports and the importance of hardening, for example. Port numbers is how we reference services and we define that services are actually applications that have been written to provide a specific service. So that's how we reference things at the transport layer through port numbers. Now these services that port numbers correspond to have been around forever. So we know which ones have vulnerabilities and so on. So this is why hardening becomes such an important thing. Here's a perfect example. I think in that last video, we did say that you don't need to memorize 65,000 odd port numbers and the services that they correspond to. That's not what makes you a competent security professional. But here's what you do need to remember, right? There are certain port numbers that equate to specific services that we know have exploits and vulnerabilities associated with them. So these are some of the ones that are most well known. You know that port 80 equates to, for example, the HTTP service, which is really the language of the web, but it does not have any sort of inherent security built in. So if you want security for HTTP traffic, it doesn't natively have it. You have to tunnel that traffic across something that can give it the security that it is lacking. And that's why we use, for example, SSL and TLS and HTTPS, for example, right? Now, some of these other ones, like for example, if you ask network people, you know, what's the most dangerous service that exists out there? A lot of them might say things like Telnet. Telnet has a lot of vulnerabilities and exploits associated with it. The idea behind hardening is that if you don't need some of these services, why would you let them run? and allow them through things like your firewall, for example. On certain hosts, obviously, it makes good sense that if you don't need those services, you turn them off so that exploits associated with them cannot be taken advantage of. The idea behind some of these, is that hardening from a network security perspective means that if you understand that some of these are exploitable, if you don't need those services, you turn them off. If you need them, then you try and go out there and find more secure versions of them. So obviously the industry has understood the importance of these things. You can't just turn off every service because then your network isn't going to function. So some of these are obviously very important to some of the services and what's going on across your network. So if you need a service, then you try and go find a more secure version of it. And here, for example, is a perfect example. FTP is port 21. It natively doesn't have any security built in. But the industry has come up with something that has more security built in for that specific service called FTP. And very appropriately, it is called SFTP. the S obviously standing for secure FTP. Now obviously we would mandate from a security perspective that we use the more secure version of it. Now certain things you can't just add security, like for example Telnet. There's no such thing as secure Telnet. But what you can do is you can tunnel your Telnet traffic across something that can give it the security that it may be lacking. So for example, we can tunnel Telnet maybe across a SSH tunnel to be able to provide things like authentication and encryption and multiplexing capabilities and some of those things. What we need to remember about this is that hardening is the concept of If we don't need a service, we turn it off. If we need it, we try and find a more secure version of it or implement the more secure version of it by tunneling it across something that can give it the security that it lacks. And then of course, hardening also means using the latest versions of things because they've been patched for known vulnerabilities and we do more monitoring and so on. But that's the idea behind some of these and the concept of hardening.
